next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The substrate went quiet around dawn.

Gavin noticed it the way you notice a headache ending — not the sudden absence of pressure but the slow realization that it had been there and now it wasn't. For hours the battle's output had saturated everything: hundreds of spells fired simultaneously, each one flexing the agreements that made it possible, the accumulated noise of it drowning out the fine structures he needed to perceive the way a brass band drowns out a whisper. He'd sat with the manuscript open on his knees and stared at notation he couldn't read because every time his perception tried to settle, another volley of combat magic dragged the substrate sideways and the patterns dissolved.

He didn't know how long the battle had lasted. Time had a different quality in the sub-collection even on normal nights — the old stone didn't carry sound from the castle above, and the self-lit walls held the same faint glow whether it was noon or midnight. But the substrate carried everything, and through it Gavin had felt the fighting as a continuous distortion, the magical equivalent of trying to read during an earthquake. He'd tried to work anyway. He'd sat on the limestone floor with his back against the shelf where he'd first found the manuscript seven months ago, and he'd held the pages open, and he'd stared at notation that churned like water over rocks every time a cluster of killing curses hit the same stretch of corridor two hundred feet above his head. He'd accomplished nothing.

And then it stopped. There was a trailing-off, a scattering of final percussiv...
open_hooks_to_carry: The battle as substrate noise: the distortion caused by hundreds of simultaneous combat spells, disrupting Gavin's delicate ward-study work. His annoyance is genuine
Navigating the battle like a crowded street: inconvenienced, not involved. The deadpan comedy of a boy walking past a war because he is thinking about ward resonance
prev_tail_excerpt:
...oice wouldn't be reversible. Adults were arriving through passages Gavin had never used — the Hog's Head tunnel, whatever routes the Order had, the channels that the DA had built over a year of careful, dangerous work. Wands were being checked. Loyalty was crystallising into the two simple categories that war demanded. The weight of the wizarding world's unresolved conflict was concentrating on this castle, tonight, and the building hummed with it.

The notation trembled. He lost his fifth parameter again.

He sat in the dry limestone cold of the sub-collection, the manuscript open before him, the quartz weights catching the faint wall-light, and he worked. The wards overhead were thin and thinning. The thing beyond them continued in its way, which was not malicious, which was not intentional, which was simply incompatible with everything that existed below the wards in the way that a rising sea was incompatible with a drawing in the sand — not through will but through presence, through the patient, geological fact of being there. The substrate flexed and hummed with the approaching battle. The notation jittered on the page.

Gavin Tench, fifteen years old, mediocre at everything except the one thing that mattered and not good enough at that, sat on the stone floor and tried to hold six variables in his mind while the world above him prepared to tear itself apart over a problem he could solve in seconds and wouldn't, because the wards were failing, and the wards were all that mattered, and the wards did not care about the war.

He turned a page. The notation moved. He read.
state_deltas_required: The Battle of Hogwarts itself — not as Gavin's fight but as interference, noise, physical danger, and the novel's climactic confrontation between Miren's demand that he act and his refusal

The novel's central ironic moment: Gavin could end the Battle of Hogwarts and does not because it is not worth the interruption to his ward work. Miren witnesses this refusal. The gap between Gavin and the human world becomes absolute
